Importing Necessary Libraries¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import time
from scipy.stats import f_oneway
import statsmodels.api as sm
from statsmodels.formula.api import ols
from scipy.stats import linregress
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import spearmanr
from scipy.stats import kruskal
import shap

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# Plotting style
sns.set(style="whitegrid")
plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (12, 6)

%matplotlib inline

Importing Dataset¶

In [2]:
df = pd.read_csv("FMCG_2022_2024.csv")

3. Invalid Values Removal¶

In [7]:
# Showing the Negative values
df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).min()
Out[7]:
price_unit          1.5
promotion_flag      0.0
delivery_days       1.0
stock_available   -12.0
delivered_qty     -11.0
units_sold        -25.0
dtype: float64
In [8]:
negative_counts = (df.select_dtypes(include='number') < 0).sum()

print("Count of negative values per column:")
print(negative_counts)

Count of negative values per column:
price_unit         0
promotion_flag     0
delivery_days      0
stock_available    3
delivered_qty      3
units_sold         3
dtype: int64
In [9]:
# Removing the Negative Rows
df = df[df['stock_available'] >= 0]
In [10]:
df.shape
Out[10]:
(190754, 14)

4. Feature Engineering¶

In [11]:
df['Revenue'] = df['units_sold'] * df['price_unit']
df['Year'] = df['date'].dt.year
df['Month'] = df['date'].dt.month
df['Day'] = df['date'].dt.day
In [12]:
# Deviding Price unit into 3 levels

df['price_range'] = pd.qcut(
    df['price_unit'],
    q=3,
    labels=['Low', 'Medium', 'High']
)

price_sales = df.groupby('price_range')['units_sold'].mean()
price_sales
Out[12]:
price_range
Low       19.956213
Medium    19.871058
High      19.933186
Name: units_sold, dtype: float64
In [13]:
# Separate units_sold by price group
Low = df[df['price_range'] == 'Low']['units_sold']
Medium = df[df['price_range'] == 'Medium']['units_sold']
High = df[df['price_range'] == 'High']['units_sold']
In [14]:
# Stock-to-Sales Ratio
df['stock_to_sales_ratio'] = df['stock_available'] / df['units_sold'].replace(0, 1)
# Inventory turnover per record
df['inventory_turnover'] = df['units_sold'] / df['stock_available'].replace(0, 1)
In [15]:
df.head()

4. Distribution Analysis¶

In [40]:
# Distribution Plots for Numerical Columns
numerical_cols = df.select_dtypes(include = np.number).columns

plt.figure(figsize = (15,10))
for i, col in enumerate(numerical_cols):
    plt.subplot(4, 4, i+1) # Adjust grid layout as needed
    sns.histplot(df[col], kde = True, bins =30)
    plt.title(col)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

5. Outlier Detection¶

In [41]:
# Outlier Detection with Boxplots
plt.figure(figsize = (15,10))
for i, col in enumerate(numerical_cols):
    plt.subplot(4, 4, i+1)
    sns.boxplot(y = df[col])
    plt.title(col)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Interpretation and Conclusion¶

Delivery Days:-¶

  1. Mean (Average):
    • The average delivery time is 3 days
    • This indicates that most deliveries are completed around 3 days
  2. Minimum = 1 day:
    • The fastest delivery recorded is 1 day, showing that quick delivery is possible in some cases.
  3. Maximum = 5 days:
    • The slowest delivery takes 5 days, indicating that delays are limited and controlled.
  4. Range (1 to 5 days):
    • Delivery times vary within a narrow and reasonable range, suggesting consistency in the delivery process.
  5. Variance = 2.001:
    • The variance shows a moderate spread of delivery days around the mean.
    • This implies that delivery times are not highly scattered.
  6. Standard Deviation = 1.414 days:
    • On average, delivery times differ from the mean by about 1.4 days.
    • Most deliveries occur between 2 to 4 days (mean ± standard deviation).

Overall Interpretation:

  • The delivery system is fairly stable and predictable.
  • Occasional early or late deliveries exist, but extreme delays are absent.

Price Unit:-¶

  1. Mean = 5.252:
    • The average price per unit is approximately 5.25.
  2. Minimum = 1.5:
    • The lowest recorded unit price is 1.5 dollars.
    • This indicates the presence of low-priced or budget items.
  3. Maximum = 9:
    • The highest unit price is 9 dollars.
    • This shows that some products are priced significantly higher than average.
  4. Range (1.5 to 9):
    • The price values span a wide range, suggesting price diversity across products.
  5. Standard Deviation = 2.167:
    • On average, unit prices deviate from the mean by about 2.17 units.
    • Most prices are likely to fall between 3.1 and 7.4 (mean ± standard deviation).
  6. Variance = 4.695:
    • The variance indicates a moderate level of dispersion in unit prices.
    • Prices are not tightly clustered, but also not extremely scattered.

Overall Interpretation

  • The Price_Unit data shows moderate variability, reflecting a mix of low-, mid-, and high-priced products.
  • This pricing structure suggests product variety and flexible pricing strategy within the dataset.

Stock Available:-¶

  1. Mean = 157.70 units:
    • On average, about 158 units of stock are available.
  2. Minimum = 0 units:
    • The minimum stock level is 0, indicating that stock-out situations occur for some items.
    • This may lead to lost sales or unmet demand.
  3. Maximum = 405 units:
    • The maximum stock available is 405 units.
    • This suggests that some products are heavily stocked, possibly high-demand items or overstocked goods.
  4. Range (0 to 405 units):
    • The stock levels show a very wide range, indicating significant variation in inventory across products.
  5. Standard Deviation = 52.73 units:
    • On average, stock levels deviate from the mean by about 53 units.
    • Most products have stock levels roughly between 105 and 210 units (mean ± standard deviation).
  6. Variance = 2780.72:
    • The high variance indicates a large dispersion in stock availability.
    • Inventory levels are highly inconsistent across different items.

Overall Interpretation

  • The stock availability data shows high variability, with both stock-outs and overstocking present.
  • This suggests a need for better inventory planning and demand forecasting.

Delivered Quantity:-¶

  1. Mean = 179.34 units:
    • On average, about 179 units are delivered per observation.
    • This represents the typical delivery quantity.
  2. Minimum = 1 unit:
    • The minimum delivered quantity is 1 unit, indicating very small or partial deliveries in some cases.
  3. Maximum = 366 units:
    • The maximum delivered quantity is 366 units.
    • This suggests that some deliveries are large-volume shipments.
  4. Range (1 to 366 units):
    • The delivered quantities span a wide range, showing variation in order sizes.
  5. Standard Deviation = 40.03 units:
    • On average, delivered quantities differ from the mean by about 40 units.
    • Most deliveries fall roughly between 139 and 219 units (mean ± standard deviation).
  6. Variance = 1602.48:
    • The variance indicates a considerable dispersion in delivered quantities.

Overall Interpretation

  • The delivered quantity shows moderate to high variability, reflecting a mix of small and large deliveries.
  • This suggests flexible delivery patterns depending on demand or order size.

Units Sold:-¶

  1. Mean = 19.92 units:
    • On average, about 20 units are sold per observation.
    • This represents the typical sales volume.
  2. Minimum = 0 units:
    • The minimum value is 0, indicating that no sales occurred in some cases.
    • This may point to low demand, stock unavailability, or inactive periods.
  3. Maximum = 139 units:
    • The maximum number of units sold is 139.
    • This shows that some observations experience very high sales, possibly due to promotions or high demand.
  4. Range (0 to 139 units):
    • The wide range suggests large variation in sales volume across observations.
  5. Standard Deviation = 11.77 units:
    • On average, sales differ from the mean by about 11.77 units.
    • Most sales values lie approximately between 8 and 32 units (mean ± standard deviation).
  6. Variance = 138.52:
    • The variance indicates a moderate level of dispersion in units sold.
    • Sales volumes are not consistent across all observations.

Overall Interpretation

  • The units_sold data shows noticeable variability, with periods of no sales and occasional high sales.
  • This reflects fluctuating demand patterns across products or time periods.

Revenue:-¶

  1. Mean = 104.59:
    • On average, the revenue generated per observation is about 104.6 Dollars.
    • This represents the typical revenue level in the dataset.
  2. Minimum = 0:
    • The minimum revenue is 0, indicating that no revenue was generated in some cases.
    • This may be due to zero sales, stock-outs, or inactive periods.
  3. Maximum = 1145.6:
    • The maximum revenue recorded is 1145.6 Dollars, which is much higher than the average.
    • This suggests the presence of very high-revenue transactions or outliers.
  4. Range (0 to 1145.6):
    • The revenue values span a very wide range, indicating large differences in revenue generation.
  5. Standard Deviation = 79.52:
    • On average, revenue deviates from the mean by about 79.5 Dollars.
    • Most revenue values are likely to fall roughly between 25 and 184 Dollars (mean ± standard deviation).
  6. Variance = 6323.81:
    • The high variance indicates substantial dispersion in revenue values.
    • Revenue is highly inconsistent across observations.

Overall Interpretation

  • The revenue data shows high variability, with many low-revenue observations and a few very high-revenue cases.
  • This suggests uneven sales performance, possibly driven by product mix, demand fluctuations, or promotions.

Overall Conclusion of Descriptive Analysis¶

  • The dataset shows a well-structured operational system with reasonably controlled delivery times, moderate pricing variability, and significant differences in inventory, sales, and revenue performance.
  • Delivery Days are fairly consistent, with an average of 3 days and a narrow range (1–5 days). This indicates a stable and predictable delivery process, with no extreme delays.
  • Price_Unit exhibits moderate variability, suggesting a mix of low-, mid-, and high-priced products. The pricing strategy appears flexible, allowing the business to cater to different customer segments.
  • Stock_Available shows high variability, ranging from 0 to 405 units. The presence of both stock-outs and overstocking highlights inefficiencies in inventory management and indicates a need for better demand forecasting and stock control.
  • Delivered Quantity varies moderately to highly, reflecting different order sizes and demand levels. This suggests that the supply chain is flexible, adjusting delivery quantities based on requirements.
  • Units_Sold displays noticeable fluctuations, with some periods of no sales and some of very high sales. This points to uneven demand patterns, possibly influenced by promotions, seasonality, or product popularity.
  • Revenue shows high dispersion, with many low-revenue observations and a few very high-revenue transactions. This indicates uneven sales performance, where revenue is heavily driven by specific products or high-volume sales events.

Final Business Insight:¶

Overall, the analysis reveals a stable delivery system and flexible pricing structure, but highlights significant variability in inventory levels, sales volume, and revenue generation. Improving inventory planning, demand forecasting, and sales strategy could help reduce inconsistencies and enhance overall business performance.

Objective 1: Sales Performance Analysis¶

1. Time-Based Sales Analysis¶

(I) Year-wise Sales Trend¶

In [42]:
Year_Frequency = df.Year.value_counts().div(1_000).round(2).astype(str) + 'K'
Year_Frequency
Out[42]:
Year
2024    83.96K
2023    77.55K
2022    29.25K
Name: count, dtype: object
In [43]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Year frequency
Year_Frequency = df['Year'].value_counts().div(1_000).round(2)

# Sort for better visualization
Year_Frequency = Year_Frequency.sort_values()

# Plot
plt.figure()
plt.barh(Year_Frequency.index.astype(str), Year_Frequency.values, color='skyblue')

plt.title("Year-wise Frequency Trend")
plt.xlabel("Count (in Thousands)")
plt.ylabel("Year")

# Show values on bars
for i, v in enumerate(Year_Frequency.values):
    plt.text(v, i, f"{v}K", va='center')

plt.show()

(II) Month-wise Sales Trend¶

In [44]:
Month_Frequency = df.Month.value_counts().sort_index().div(1_000).round(1).astype(str) + 'K'

In [45]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Month frequency
Month_Frequency = df['Month'].value_counts().sort_index().div(1_000).round(1)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))

# Line plot with markers
plt.plot(
    Month_Frequency.index,
    Month_Frequency.values,
    marker='o',
    linewidth=2
)

plt.title("Month-wise Frequency Trend")
plt.xlabel("Month")
plt.ylabel("Count (in Thousands)")

# Grid for better readability
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)

# Value labels
for x, y in zip(Month_Frequency.index, Month_Frequency.values):
    plt.text(x, y, f"{y}K", ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=9)

plt.show()

In [47]:
Day_Frequency = df.Day.value_counts().sort_index().div(1_000).round(1)

plt.figure(figsize=(20,6))

plt.plot(
    Day_Frequency.index,
    Day_Frequency.values,
    marker='o'
)

plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.9)
plt.title("Day-wise Frequency Trend (Day 1–31)")
plt.xlabel("Day")
plt.ylabel("Count (in Thousands)")

# Value labels
for x, y in zip(Day_Frequency.index, Day_Frequency.values):
    plt.text(x, y, f"{y}K", ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=8)

plt.show()

2. Sales Distribution Across Dimensions¶

(I) Region-wise Sales Distribution¶

In [48]:
df.groupby('region')['units_sold'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False).div(1_000_000).round(3).astype(str) + 'M'
Out[48]:
region
PL-North       1.27M
PL-South      1.268M
PL-Central    1.262M
Name: units_sold, dtype: object
In [49]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Data already prepared
sales_by_region = (df.groupby('region')['units_sold'].sum().div(1_000_000).round(3))

# Professional color palette
colors = ['#2E86AB', '#A23B72', '#F18F01', '#C73E1D', '#6A994E']
# Alternative professional palettes:
# colors = ['#264653', '#2A9D8F', '#E9C46A', '#F4A261', '#E76F51']
# colors = ['#1B3B4F', '#2A5F6E', '#4C8577', '#B5B682', '#F4D58C']

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 7))

# Create donut chart with enhanced styling
wedges, texts, autotexts = plt.pie(
    sales_by_region.values,
    labels=sales_by_region.index,
    autopct=lambda pct: f"{round(pct/100.*sales_by_region.sum(), 3)}M",
    startangle=90,
    colors=colors[:len(sales_by_region)],
    wedgeprops={'edgecolor': 'white', 'linewidth': 2, 'antialiased': True},
    textprops={'fontsize': 12, 'fontweight': 'bold'},
    pctdistance=0.85,
    labeldistance=1.1
)

# Style the percentage text inside the donut
for autotext in autotexts:
    autotext.set_color('white')
    autotext.set_fontsize(11)
    autotext.set_fontweight('bold')
    autotext.set_bbox(dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.3', facecolor='black', alpha=0.7, edgecolor='none'))

# Style the region labels outside the donut
for text in texts:
    text.set_color('#2C3E50')
    text.set_fontsize(11)
    text.set_fontweight('semibold')

# Draw center circle (donut hole) with optional inner text
centre_circle = plt.Circle((0, 0), 0.70, fc='white', edgecolor='#E0E0E0', linewidth=2)
fig = plt.gcf()
fig.gca().add_artist(centre_circle)

# Add total sales in the center
total_sales = sales_by_region.sum()
plt.text(0, 0, f'Total\n{total_sales:.1f}M', 
         ha='center', va='center', 
         fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', 
         color='#2C3E50')

# Add title and styling
plt.title('Sales Distribution by Region', 
          fontsize=16, fontweight='bold', 
          pad=20, color='#2C3E50')

# Add subtle shadow effect
for wedge in wedges:
    wedge.set_edgecolor('white')
    wedge.set_linewidth(2)

# Equal aspect ratio ensures the pie is circular
plt.axis('equal')

# Add legend
plt.legend(wedges, sales_by_region.index,
           title="Regions",
           loc="center left",
           bbox_to_anchor=(1, 0, 0.5, 1),
           fontsize=10,
           title_fontsize=11)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

(II) Channel-wise Sales Distribution¶

df.groupby('channel')['units_sold'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False).div(1_000_000).round(3).astype(str) + 'M'

channel

Discount      1.264M

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Data already prepared
sales_by_channel = (df.groupby('channel')['units_sold'].sum().div(1_000_000).round(3))

# Map colors based on available channels
colors = ['#264653', '#2A9D8F', '#E9C46A']

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 7))

# Create donut chart with enhanced styling
wedges, texts, autotexts = plt.pie(
    sales_by_channel.values,
    labels=sales_by_channel.index,
    autopct=lambda pct: f"{round(pct/100.*sales_by_channel.sum(), 3)}M",
    startangle=90,
    colors=colors,
    wedgeprops={'edgecolor': 'white', 'linewidth': 2, 'antialiased': True},
    textprops={'fontsize': 12, 'fontweight': 'bold'},
    pctdistance=0.85,
    labeldistance=1.1
)

# Style the percentage text inside the donut
for autotext in autotexts:
    autotext.set_color('white')
    autotext.set_fontsize(11)
    autotext.set_fontweight('bold')
    autotext.set_bbox(dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.3', facecolor='black', alpha=0.7, edgecolor='none'))

# Style the channel labels outside the donut
for text in texts:
    text.set_color('#2C3E50')
    text.set_fontsize(11)
    text.set_fontweight('semibold')

# Draw center circle (donut hole) with styling
centre_circle = plt.Circle((0, 0), 0.70, fc='white', edgecolor='#E0E0E0', linewidth=2)
fig = plt.gcf()
fig.gca().add_artist(centre_circle)

# Add total sales in the center
total_sales = sales_by_channel.sum()
plt.text(0, 0, f'Total\n{total_sales:.1f}M', 
         ha='center', va='center', 
         fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', 
         color='#2C3E50')

# Add title with professional styling
plt.title('Sales Performance by Channel', 
          fontsize=16, fontweight='bold', 
          pad=20, color='#2C3E50')

# Add subtle shadow effect
for wedge in wedges:
    wedge.set_edgecolor('white')
    wedge.set_linewidth(2)

# Equal aspect ratio ensures the pie is circular
plt.axis('equal')

# Add legend
plt.legend(wedges, sales_by_channel.index,
           title="Channels",
           loc="center left",
           bbox_to_anchor=(1, 0, 0.5, 1),
           fontsize=10,
           title_fontsize=11)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Numeric values in millions
pack_sales = (df.groupby('pack_type')['units_sold'].sum().div(1_000_000))

# Option 6: Sunset Palette

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 7))

# Create donut chart with enhanced styling 
wedges, texts, autotexts = plt.pie(
    pack_sales.values,
    labels=pack_sales.index,
    autopct=lambda pct: f"{round((pct/100)*pack_sales.sum(), 3)}M",
    startangle=90,
    colors=colors[:len(pack_sales)],
    wedgeprops={'edgecolor': 'white', 'linewidth': 2, 'antialiased': True},
    textprops={'fontsize': 12, 'fontweight': 'bold'},
    pctdistance=0.85,
    labeldistance=1.1
)

# Style the percentage text inside the donut
for autotext in autotexts:
    autotext.set_color('white')
    autotext.set_fontsize(11)
    autotext.set_fontweight('bold')
    autotext.set_bbox(dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.3', facecolor='black', alpha=0.7, edgecolor='none'))

# Style the pack type labels outside the donut
for text in texts:
    text.set_color('#2C3E50')
    text.set_fontsize(11)
    text.set_fontweight('semibold')

# Draw center circle (donut hole) with styling
centre_circle = plt.Circle((0, 0), 0.70, fc='white', edgecolor='#E0E0E0', linewidth=2)
fig = plt.gcf()
fig.gca().add_artist(centre_circle)

# Add total sales in the center
total_sales = pack_sales.sum()
plt.text(0, 0, f'Total\n{total_sales:.1f}M', 
         ha='center', va='center', 
         fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', 
         color='#2C3E50')

# Add title with professional styling
plt.title('Sales Performance by Pack Type', 
          fontsize=16, fontweight='bold', 
          pad=20, color='#2C3E50')

# Add subtle shadow effect
for wedge in wedges:
    wedge.set_edgecolor('white')
    wedge.set_linewidth(2)

# Equal aspect ratio ensures the pie is circular
plt.axis('equal')

# Add legend
plt.legend(wedges, pack_sales.index,
           title="Pack Types",
           loc="center left",
           bbox_to_anchor=(1, 0, 0.5, 1),
           fontsize=10,
           title_fontsize=11)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

4. Contribution Analysis¶

(I) Category Contribution (%)¶

In [58]:
# Total units sold
total_units_sold = df['units_sold'].sum()

# Category-wise units sold contribution
category_contribution = (
    df.groupby('category')['units_sold'].sum() / total_units_sold * 100
).sort_values(ascending=False)

# Add % sign
category_contribution_percent = category_contribution.round(2).astype(str) + '%'

print(category_contribution_percent)

# Total units sold
total_units = df['units_sold'].sum()

# Contribution calculation
brand_contribution = (
    df.groupby('brand')['units_sold']
    .sum()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .div(total_units)
    .mul(100)
    .round(2)
    .astype(str) + '%'
)

brand_contribution

In [61]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Calculate contribution (numeric)
total_units = df['units_sold'].sum()

brand_contribution = (
    df.groupby('brand')['units_sold']
    .sum()
    .sort_values(ascending=True)   # ascending for horizontal bar
    / total_units * 100
)

# Create colors (highlight top 5)
colors = ['green' if i >= len(brand_contribution)-5 else 'lightgray' 
          for i in range(len(brand_contribution))]

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
plt.barh(brand_contribution.index, brand_contribution.values, color=colors)

plt.title("Top Brand Contribution Highlighted")
plt.xlabel("Contribution (%)")
plt.ylabel("Brand")

# Value labels
for i, v in enumerate(brand_contribution.values):
    plt.text(v, i, f"{v:.2f}%", va='center')

plt.show()

Objective 2: Price Impact on Sales¶

Correlation Analysis (Pearson)¶

In [70]:
correlation = df['price_unit'].corr(df['units_sold'])
print("Correlation between Price and Sales:", correlation)

Correlation between Price and Sales: -0.0010850357751209553
In [71]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.scatter(df['price_unit'], df['units_sold'])
plt.title('Price vs Units Sold')
plt.xlabel('Price per Unit')
plt.ylabel('Units Sold')
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.show()

Spearman’s Rank Correlation Coefficient Test¶

Assumptions of Spearman’s Rank Correlation Coefficient Test¶

  1. Variables are at least Ordinal

  • The variables should be ordinal, interval, or ratio.

  • Data must be capable of being ranked.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

  1. Monotonic Relationship

  • The relationship between the two variables should be monotonic:

    • As one variable increases, the other consistently increases or decreases.

  • The relationship does not have to be linear.

❌ This assumption is not satisfied.

  1. Paired Observations

  • Each observation in one variable must have a corresponding observation in the other variable.

  • Data should be measured on the same set of cases.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

  1. Independence of Observations

  • Each pair of observations must be independent of all other pairs.

  • No repeated or related measurements.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

  1. No Requirement of Normality

  • Spearman’s method does NOT assume normal distribution of variables.

  • This makes it suitable for skewed data.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

  1. Robust to Outliers

  • The method is less sensitive to outliers because it uses ranks instead of raw values.

  • Extreme values should not dominate the relationship.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

Conclusion:

  • Here our all assumptions satisfied except Assumption no. 2 which is Monotonic Relationship but still we could use Spearman’s Rank Correlation Coefficient Test because it does not affect the result that much.
In [72]:
# Spearman correlation
corr, p_value = spearmanr(df['price_unit'], df['units_sold'])

print("Spearman Correlation Coefficient:", corr)
print("P-value:", p_value)

Spearman Correlation Coefficient: -0.0008915315272088063
P-value: 0.6969969664572935

Kruskal-Wallis Test¶

Assumption of Kruskal-Wallis Test¶

  1. Independent Samples

  • The groups being compared must be independent of each other.

  • No observation should belong to more than one group.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

  1. Ordinal or Continuous Dependent Variable

  • The dependent variable should be ordinal, interval, or ratio.

  • Data must be suitable for ranking.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

  1. Categorical Independent Variable

  • The independent variable must be categorical with two or more groups.

  • Groups can be of unequal size.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

  1. Similar Distribution Shapes

  • The distributions of the dependent variable across groups should have a similar shape.

  • If shapes differ greatly, the test may reflect distribution differences rather than medians.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

  1. Random Sampling
  • Observations should be randomly sampled from the population.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

  1. No Normality Assumption

  • The Kruskal–Wallis test does NOT require normal distribution.

  • Suitable for skewed data and outliers.

✅ This assumption is satisfied.

Conclusion:

  • Here our all observation are satisfied so we could use Kruskal-Wallis Test.
In [73]:
sns.boxplot(x='price_range', y='units_sold', data=df)
plt.title("Units Sold by Price Group")
plt.xlabel("Price Group")
plt.ylabel("Units Sold")
plt.show()
In [74]:
plt.figure()
plt.bar(price_sales.index, price_sales.values, color = ['#a05195','#ffa600','#4D96FF'])
plt.title('Average Sales by Price Range')
plt.xlabel('Price Range')
plt.ylabel('Average Units Sold')
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.show()
In [75]:
stat, p_value = kruskal(Low, Medium, High)

print("Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared statistic: ", stat)
print("P-Value: ", p_value)

Kruskal-Wallis chi-squared statistic:  0.34793343688229883
P-Value:  0.8403248645144705

Objective 3: Effect of Promotion on Sales¶

1. Descriptive Comparison of Promoted vs Non-Promoted Products¶

In [76]:
comparison = (
    df.groupby('promotion_flag')
      .agg(avg_units_sold=('units_sold', 'mean'),
           avg_revenue=('Revenue', 'mean'))
      .round(2)
)

print(comparison)

                avg_units_sold  avg_revenue
promotion_flag                             
0                        17.44        91.50
1                        34.06       179.26
In [77]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

comparison.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Average Units Sold and Revenue: Promoted vs Non-Promoted')
plt.xlabel('Promotion Status')
plt.ylabel('Average Value')
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.show()

2. Distribution Comparison Using Boxplots¶

In [78]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure()
df.boxplot(column='units_sold', by='promotion_flag')
plt.title('Units Sold: Promoted vs Non-Promoted')
plt.suptitle('')
plt.xlabel('Promotion (0 = No, 1 = Yes)')
plt.ylabel('Units Sold')
plt.show()

<Figure size 1200x600 with 0 Axes>

3. Mann–Whitney U Test¶

In [79]:
from scipy.stats import mannwhitneyu

# Separate groups
promoted = df[df['promotion_flag'] == 1]['units_sold']
non_promoted = df[df['promotion_flag'] == 0]['units_sold']

# Test for units_sold
stat, p = mannwhitneyu(promoted, non_promoted)

print("Mann-Whitney U Test (Units Sold)")
print("Statistic:", stat)
print("p-value:", p)

Mann-Whitney U Test (Units Sold)
Statistic: 3834404037.5
p-value: 0.0

In [80]:

    df.groupby(['region', 'promotion_flag'])['units_sold']
      .mean()
      .unstack()
)

region_promo.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Average Units Sold by Region and Promotion')
plt.xlabel('Region')
plt.ylabel('Average Units Sold')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

plt.show()

<Figure size 1200x600 with 0 Axes>

4. Units sold Comparison¶

In [87]:
 # Group units_sold by channel
channel_groups_units = [
    group['units_sold'].values 
    for name, group in df.groupby('channel')
]

# Perform test
h_stat_units, p_value_units = kruskal(*channel_groups_units)

print("Kruskal–Wallis Test (Units Sold Across Channels)")
print("H-statistic:", h_stat_units)
print("p-value:", p_value_units)

Kruskal–Wallis Test (Units Sold Across Channels)
H-statistic: 0.12428629037911208
p-value: 0.9397483567059958

Interpretation (Based on 5% Significance Level)¶

In [103]:
alpha = 0.05

if p_value_units < alpha:
    print("\nReject H₀: At least one channel differs significantly in Units Sold.")
else:
    print("\nFail to Reject H₀: No significant difference in Units Sold across channels.")

Fail to Reject H₀: No significant difference in Units Sold across channels.

5. Effect Size Analysis¶

In [97]:
import numpy as np

# overall mean
overall_mean = df['units_sold'].mean()

# between group sum of squares
ss_between = sum(
    len(group) * (group['units_sold'].mean() - overall_mean)**2
    for name, group in df.groupby('channel')
)

# total sum of squares
ss_total = sum((df['units_sold'] - overall_mean)**2)

eta_squared = ss_between / ss_total

print("Effect Size (Eta Squared):", eta_squared)

Effect Size (Eta Squared): 2.292031341291884e-06

6. Revenue Comparison¶

In [100]:
channel_groups_rev = [
    group['Revenue'].values 
    for name, group in df.groupby('channel')
]

h_stat_rev, p_value_rev = kruskal(*channel_groups_rev)

print("Kruskal–Wallis Test (Revenue)")
print("H-statistic:", h_stat_rev)
print("p-value:", p_value_rev)

Kruskal–Wallis Test (Revenue)
H-statistic: 0.3701838173882062
p-value: 0.8310279016528812

Interpretation (Based on 5% Significance Level)¶

In [104]:
alpha = 0.05

if p_value_rev < alpha:
    print("Reject H₀: At least one channel differs significantly in Revenue.")
else:
    print("Fail to Reject H₀: No significant difference in Revenue across channels.")

Fail to Reject H₀: No significant difference in Revenue across channels.

7. Consistency Analysis (CV)¶

In [98]:
cv = df.groupby('channel')['units_sold'].std() / df.groupby('channel')['units_sold'].mean()

print("Coefficient of Variation:")
print(cv)

Coefficient of Variation:
channel
Discount      0.590821
E-commerce    0.592797
Retail        0.588861
Name: units_sold, dtype: float64

Objective 5: Inventory & Stock Efficiency¶

1. Descriptive Analysis¶

In [90]:
# Summary statistics
stock_sales_summary = df[['stock_available', 'units_sold']].describe()
print(stock_sales_summary)

       stock_available     units_sold
count    190754.000000  190754.000000
mean        157.700237      19.920211
std          52.732487      11.769429
min           0.000000       0.000000
25%         124.000000      12.000000
50%         155.000000      18.000000
75%         192.000000      25.000000
max         405.000000     139.000000

2. Inventory Efficiency Metrics¶

(I) Stock-to-Sales Ratio¶

In [91]:
# Average ratio
avg_stock_sales_ratio = df['stock_to_sales_ratio'].mean()

print("Average Stock-to-Sales Ratio:", round(avg_stock_sales_ratio, 2))

Average Stock-to-Sales Ratio: 9.18

Interpretation:¶

The average stock-to-sales ratio of 9.18 indicates that inventory levels are significantly higher than sales volume, suggesting potential overstocking and inefficient inventory management.

(II) Inventory Turnover Ratio¶

In [92]:
# Average turnover
avg_turnover = df['inventory_turnover'].mean()

print("Average Inventory Turnover Ratio:", round(avg_turnover, 2))

Average Inventory Turnover Ratio: 0.12

Interpretation:¶

The average inventory turnover ratio of 0.12 indicates slow stock movement, suggesting excess inventory and possible inefficiencies in demand forecasting and inventory planning.

3. Visual Analysis¶

Scatter Plot (Stock vs Sales)¶

In [93]:
plt.figure()
plt.scatter(df['stock_available'], df['units_sold'])
plt.title('Stock Available vs Units Sold')
plt.xlabel('Stock Available')
plt.ylabel('Units Sold')
plt.show()

4. Correlation Analysis¶

Spearman Correlation¶

In [95]:
spearman_corr, spearman_p = stats.spearmanr(df['stock_available'], df['units_sold'])

print("\nSpearman Correlation")
print("Correlation Coefficient:", round(spearman_corr, 3))
print("p-value:", spearman_p)

Spearman Correlation
Correlation Coefficient: 0.638
p-value: 0.0
